entries.tutorialSubtitle: American spiced layer cake with cream cheese frosting
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Applesauce cake is an American autumn cake: a moist, warmly spiced sponge made with stewed apple purée in place of some of the fat, giving it a denser, tender crumb. It is baked as two 20 cm layers and filled and frosted with a sharp cream cheese icing that cuts through the sweetness. The apple flavour is in the background; what comes forward is cinnamon, nutmeg, and the slight tang of the frosting.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Egyptian semolina cake soaked in rose water syrup
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Basbousa is a dense, moist Egyptian semolina cake baked in a tray and soaked immediately in cold sugar syrup while still hot. The contrast of hot cake and cold syrup is what drives the syrup deep into the crumb. It is cut into diamonds or squares in the tin and eaten at room temperature. The crumb is slightly grainy from the semolina and the flavour is plain and sweet, lifted by the rose water.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Turkish yeasted flatbread cooked on the hob
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Bazlama is a thick, soft Turkish flatbread, yeasted and slightly tangy, cooked in a dry pan on the hob. It is thicker and chewier than lavash, closer in texture to a thick pitta but without the pocket. In Turkey it is sold warm from griddles at market stalls; at home it comes together in well under two hours and is best eaten within a few hours of baking.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Swiss-style brioche filled with vanilla pastry cream and chocolate chips
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Brioche suisse are individual pastries from Swiss and French pâtisseries: a thin sheet of brioche dough spread with vanilla pastry cream and scattered with chocolate chips, then folded over itself, cut into rectangles, and proved until pillowy. Baked, they peel apart in layers, giving alternating panels of soft brioche, creamy filling, and melted chocolate in each bite. They are a patisserie pastry that can be made at home with one long session on the day before.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Classic buttermilk scones with dark chocolate chips
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Chocolate chip scones: a classic buttermilk scone with dark chocolate chips worked through the dough. They are richer than a plain scone, not sweet enough to be a cake, and best eaten warm with a little clotted cream or just as they are. The buttermilk gives them a slight tang and helps the lift. The dark chocolate chips melt partially during baking, creating small pockets of softness in the crumb.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Mexican coconut sweets with condensed milk
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Cocadas are a simple Mexican coconut sweet made by stirring desiccated coconut into condensed milk with a little egg, then spooning onto a tray and baking until the edges are golden and the centres are set but still chewy. They are barely a recipe (the whole process takes fifteen minutes) but the result is addictive: intensely sweet, coconutty, with a slight chew from the egg. They keep for several days in a tin.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Sicilian filled fig and date cookies
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Cuccidati are Sicilian Christmas cookies: a short, buttery pastry wrapped around a filling of dried fruit, nuts, and warm spices, baked until lightly golden, then glazed with a thin icing. The filling is dense and dark, fragrant with orange zest, cinnamon, and cloves. In Sicily they are made with dried figs; this version uses medjool dates, which are widely available and carry the same deep sweetness. They keep for weeks in a tin, which is why they are Christmas food.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: British cinder toffee with bicarbonate of soda
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Honeycomb is made from sugar and golden syrup cooked to hard crack stage, then taken off the heat and hit with bicarbonate of soda, which causes an immediate and violent foam. The mixture is poured at once (don't touch it) and within minutes it sets into a brittle, caramel-tasting slab riddled with air bubbles. It shatters when you snap it. It is the inside of a Crunchie bar, and it is easy to make once you understand that the bicarbonate foam can't be rushed or stirred down.

entries.tutorialExcerpt: Palets Bretons are the individual biscuit form of Breton butter baking: thick, round, deeply flavoured with butter and salt, with a slight crunch on the outside and a short, sandy crumb inside. They are closer to a thick sablé than to an English biscuit; denser and more cake-like than a digestive, more robust than a shortbread. entries.tutorialSubtitle: The dense, flat bread roll of northeast England
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The stottie is the bread of the northeast: a thick, flat disc, heavy in the hand, with a tight crumb and a very slight chew. It comes from the practice of using up leftover dough; flattened and baked on the bottom of the oven. The result is a roll that holds a filling without becoming soggy. Pease pudding and cooked ham are the traditional fillings, but any sturdy filling works.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Shortcrust tart with mixed mushrooms, gruyère, and thyme
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A deep, mushroom-heavy quiche in a shortcrust case: chestnut and oyster mushrooms fried with shallots, garlic, and thyme, joined by soaked dried porcini for depth, then set in a cream and egg custard with gruyère. The custard should be just set and trembling slightly at the centre when it comes out of the oven; it firms as it cools. Serve warm or at room temperature. It slices cleanly once it has rested for 20 minutes.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Australian custard-flavoured sandwich biscuits
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Yo-yo biscuits are a classic Australian biscuit: soft, pale, custard-flavoured rounds sandwiched with a thick custard buttercream. They are made with a high proportion of custard powder, which gives both the biscuit and the filling their characteristic pale yellow colour and vanilla-custard flavour. They are not crisp like a shortbread; they have a soft, melt-in-the-mouth crumb. They are better the next day once the filling softens the biscuit slightly.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Swiss Sunday braided loaf with a glossy butter-enriched crumb
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Zopf is the bread Swiss families make on Sunday: a three- or four-strand braid, buttery and soft, with a fine crumb and an eggwash gloss. It sits at the table throughout the day. The dough is enriched but not as heavy as brioche; the butter is folded in cold, which gives the crumb a layered, almost flaky quality when you pull it apart.
